examples.title: Parallel Reduction for Array Sum
examples.description: OpenMP program demonstrating reduction clause to compute sum of array elements in parallel.
examples.explanation: Each thread gets a private copy of 'sum' initialized to 0. Threads compute partial sums for their assigned iterations. At the implicit barrier, partial sums are combined using the + operator. The final result is stored in the original 'sum' variable. The loop index 'i' is automatically private.
examples.timeComplexity: O(N/P + log P) where P is thread count
examples.spaceComplexity: O(P) for private sum copies + O(1) additional space

examples.title: Producer-Consumer with Critical Sections
examples.description: Implementation of producer-consumer pattern using OpenMP critical sections for thread-safe buffer operations.
examples.explanation: Two producers and two consumers operate on a shared bounded buffer. The 'critical' sections ensure mutual exclusion - only one thread can execute the buffer operations at a time, preventing race conditions. Note: This is a simplified implementation; in practice, condition variables would be used for efficient blocking rather than busy-waiting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(NUM_ITEMS) per producer/consumer with serialization overhead
examples.spaceComplexity: O(BUFFER_SIZE) for shared buffer + O(1) per thread

examples.title: Task-Based Parallel Quicksort
examples.description: OpenMP task directive for parallelizing quicksort recursively, demonstrating dynamic task creation and synchronization.
examples.explanation: The quicksort algorithm naturally creates independent subproblems ideal for task parallelism. The '#pragma omp task' creates explicit work units for recursive calls that can be executed by any available thread. The '#pragma omp taskwait' ensures both subproblems complete before returning, maintaining correctness. This approach handles irregular parallelism efficiently.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) average case, work divided among P threads
examples.spaceComplexity: O(log n) call stack depth, O(P) task queue potential
